sentence: Local Authorities, Powers of—Freedom of Contract, proposed, 393, 401, 407, 412, 418, 425, 428, 432, 433.
haiku_v2_rationale: This is a contents/index entry listing page numbers for a topic about freedom of contract for local authorities; it makes no substantive claim about liberty as a value.

sentence: She has won this freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence refers to winning "this freedom" without specifying what freedom is at issue or whether it concerns liberation from constraint or empowerment to act. The referent of "this freedom" is not defined within the sentence itself.

sentence: Where these students are entrusted with freedom it is not abused, but in the very exceptional case.
haiku_v2_rationale: Students are entrusted with freedom (from external control/oversight), which is a capacity they are granted or allowed to exercise without interference or abuse.

sentence: I am aware, of course, that concern about freedom of information derives in some cases more from genuine conviction than simple political opportunism.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of information is the freedom from government interference with access to information—a classic negative liberty concerning the ability to access and receive information without state restraint.

sentence: The Catalonian parliamentarians have the right to freedom of expression when they debate the question of independence, which they have done, so I do not really see why the hon.
haiku_v2_rationale: Catalonian parliamentarians are freed from interference with their expression/debate on the question of independence—this invokes freedom from restraint on political speech.

sentence: Nobody in this House is more in sympathy with the desire for self-government and freedom than those who are supporting this Motion.
haiku_v2_rationale: Self-government is a positive liberty claim: it concerns empowering a people/nation to govern themselves, not merely freedom from external interference.

sentence: Many have been led to believe that the Bill will contain stronger and better methods of safeguarding the liberty of the individual.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence concerns safeguarding the liberty of the individual—that is, protecting freedom from interference and intrusion. "Safeguarding liberty" is protecting the individual's freedom from wrongful constraint.

sentence: Those principles include our belief in free markets, our belief in private enterprise and in personal freedom and personal responsibility and the reintroduction of proper deterrence and punishment into the law and order system.
haiku_v2_rationale: Personal freedom is being freed from state interference; the speaker invokes it as a core principle alongside free markets and private enterprise, which collectively represent a freedom-from-intrusion philosophy.

sentence: and that instead of tearing each other to pieces with a waste of blood, such as few wars have occasioned, some compromise could have been effected, favourable at once to rational principles of liberty, and to the peace of the old world and the new!
haiku_v2_rationale: What is being freed: human societies and nations. From/to what: from violent warfare and toward peaceful coexistence governed by rational principles of liberty (freedom from despotic interference). The sentence invokes liberty as a value opposed to the bloodshed of war, identifying it as absence of external coercion.

sentence: and his Ministers thought of the meetings which shouted for "Wilkes and Liberty"?
haiku_v2_rationale: "Wilkes and Liberty" is a rhetorical invocation of liberty as a political cause opposing government tyranny and oppression, referring to the 18th-century Wilkes affair—a classic demand for freedom from unlawful interference with speech and parliamentary representation.

sentence: The rights and the freedoms of, and our responsibilities in this country to, the people of Hong Kong should not be sacrificed on the altar of bankers’ bonuses.
haiku_v2_rationale: Rights and freedoms of Hong Kong people are being protected from sacrifice (interference/abandonment) for economic interests; this invokes liberty as a value against instrumental harm.

sentence: With regard to the circulation of Bank paper, he would take the liberty of making one remark;
haiku_v2_rationale: This is parliamentary procedure language: "took the liberty of" is an idiomatic formula meaning "I will permit myself to" or "I will proceed to," not a substantive claim about liberty-as-a-value.

sentence: The laws of arrest mark the legal balance between civil liberty and compulsory powers for the institution of a criminal process.
haiku_v2_rationale: The individual is freed (from arbitrary arrest/detention without legal process) by legal constraints on arrest powers — a negative liberty interest (protection from state interference) being discussed as balanced against state's compulsory powers.

sentence: They are such as I think are conducive to the general good, and the liberty and happiness of my fellow-subjects.
haiku_v2_rationale: Liberty is invoked as a general value opposed to what would harm the common good and subjects' happiness; the speaker claims their measures protect liberty as a condition of human flourishing, without specifying freedom from particular constraints or enabling particular capacities.

sentence: It used to be the characteristic policy of England throughout Europe to aid the cause of liberty and independence—to cherish the feelings of patriotism and love of freedom, to restrain the strong, to assist the weak, to resist the encroachments of despotism, and to afford a refuge to the destitute and oppressed.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom is being freed from despotism, encroachment, and oppression—England's policy aimed to restrain tyrannical power and resist external constraints on nations' independence.

sentence: That Conference passed a resolution, he thought with absolute unanimity, urging that liberty should be accorded to the colonies to make fiscal arrangements without reference to any foreign Power.
haiku_v2_rationale: What is being freed: the colonies' capacity to make fiscal arrangements independently. From-or-to: empowerment/grant of authority to govern themselves without external interference or constraint.

sentence: This whole area is one where we have to ensure that the liberal values, to support freedom but not the freedom to harm others, that we apply through legislation to many other  parts of our lives are brought to bear on the online world as well.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom from harm (negative liberty) in balance with freedom to act without interference (negative liberty), where legislation enables that balance across domains including online conduct.

sentence: He read it before it was in print, and had come to the conclusion it was the only way by which they could secure the liberty of the subject and the chance of expressing views which happened to be hostile to those entertained by either the majority or the minority of the audience.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom to express views (the specific object) that are hostile to those of the majority or minority—this is freedom from interference with expression, a classic negative liberty claim about the right to speak dissenting opinions without restraint.

sentence: The oil industries are glad to feel that until last year there was comparative freedom from strikes.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom from strikes is freedom from unwanted interference with production and operations—a negative liberty claim about absence of disruption.

sentence: Throughout the realm of human rights, the concepts of personal liberty and freedom which all hon.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "personal liberty and freedom" as foundational human rights concepts without specifying whether they concern freedom from interference or empowered capacity to act.

sentence:
I put it to you, Sir, as a question of order, whether he is at liberty to make that statement
 330 
without announcing that the Government intend to move to rescind the Resolution for the appointment of the Committee.
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "at liberty to" is a parliamentary procedural idiom meaning "permitted" or "allowed," used to question whether a member is procedurally permitted to make a statement without prior disclosure. This is not substantive political liberty-as-a-value.

sentence: I said then that the Good Friday-Belfast agreement was not just about Northern Ireland, Ireland or a border, but the freedom of movement of people across these islands and the deep roots we have.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "freedom of movement of people" as a core value of the Good Friday-Belfast agreement, designating movement as a freedom from state interference rather than an empowered capacity.

sentence: We are desirous of extending in every possible way every possible freedom to trade that we can, consistently with a due regard to the interests of the revenue and the interest of the fair dealer.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence seeks to extend freedom of trade — freedom from interference with commercial activity — though subject to revenue and fair-dealing constraints.

sentence: Will that man be disposed to look kindly on the class legislation that thus abridges his liberty?
haiku_v2_rationale: A man's liberty is being abridged by class legislation; abridging liberty means restricting his freedom from interference or constraint.

sentence: We are now getting a very clear idea of freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence makes a bare invocation of "freedom" without specifying what is being freed, from what, or to what; the context is unavailable and the sentence does not disambiguate whether the speaker means negative liberty (freedom from interference) or positive liberty (empowered capacity).

sentence: The Duke of Edinburgh Award gave them the freedom and inspiration to do something.
haiku_v2_rationale: The Duke of Edinburgh Award enabled participants (capacity/empowerment) to do something unspecified—freedom here means the opportunity and inspiration granted to act, not freedom from interference.

sentence: Writing on the 6th of May, 1856, one of the men still living who were responsible for that war pointed out the future danger of an "encroachment upon and absorption of Turkey by Russia," adding that "such a danger to the peace, liberty, and privileges of all Europe we were bound to resist by every means in our power.
haiku_v2_rationale: Peace, liberty, and privileges of Europe are being freed from encroachment and absorption by Russia, a constraint on European independence and self-governance.

sentence: You have equally vocal throughout the same countries a passionate desire for freedom and for justice.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence expresses a passionate desire for freedom as a cause/value alongside justice, without specifying what is being freed or from-or-to what. It is a rhetorical invocation of freedom as an abstract ideal without the concrete specification required to distinguish negative from positive liberty.

sentence: If it is against the doctrine, why not have freedom of conscience as well?
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes "freedom of conscience" as a principle protecting individuals from interference in their moral and ethical beliefs, in the context of questioning whether a doctrine should be enforced or allowed.

sentence: When we interfered by the Poor Law in Ireland, we did not interfere with the freedom of contract.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker asserts that the Poor Law intervention did not restrict freedom of contract—the object of freedom is contract, and the claim is about non-interference with it.

sentence: They presumed him to have attained a degree of civilization from which he was yet far removed, that he had acquired those habits which would render him an industrious labourer if he were free, and that he regarded freedom with the same feeling as they did, instead of considering it, as he really did, in no other light than as a total cessation from any employment.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom is being understood as cessation from employment (release from labor obligation), contrasted with what European civilization would have cultivated (industriousness). The object being freed is the enslaved person from the condition of labor itself.

sentence: Since the Prime Minister has often declared support for open government, may I ask the Leader of the House when the Government will bring forward their Freedom of Information Bill?
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "Freedom of Information" as a legislative initiative—the freedom to access government information without bureaucratic interference, a classic negative liberty claim.

sentence: Their argument was that for many years the Business of the House had been increasing, and the conclusion they drew was that instead of maintaining the liberty of the subject in proportion to the increase of Business, the facilities for control should be diminished.
haiku_v2_rationale: The subject being freed is "the subject" (the citizen), who should be freed from parliamentary control and interference—a non-interference concept—as the House's business grows.

sentence: In the Second World War he became Prime Minister again and led his country into the fight for democracy and freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: The country is being freed from totalitarian/authoritarian rule (Nazi oppression) through military action in defence of democratic self-governance—a classic rhetorical invocation of negative liberty against tyranny.

sentence: Baronet and the other noted apostles of human liberty who support it, have been longing for a funeral that has not taken place.
haiku_v2_rationale: Human liberty is invoked here as a cause or ideal (contrasted with something requiring a "funeral"), functioning as a rhetorical appeal to freedom from constraint or tyranny, typical of negative liberty discourse.

sentence: and she said that she felt sure that the system of Government regulation of vice and examination of women is contrary not only to the rights of woman, but to the general liberty of the State.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence identifies "liberty of the State" as being threatened by government regulation and examination of women — freedom from state interference in personal/bodily matters.

sentence:
Lord Stowell
 1696 
laid down the rule which prevailed in his time in Doctors' Commons on the subject in these words—
 "This Court considers a private separation as an illegal contract, implying a dereliction of stipulated duties, which the parties are not at liberty to desert.
haiku_v2_rationale: Parties are not at liberty to desert their stipulated duties in marriage contracts—this is freedom from interference with contractual obligation, a constraint on liberty of contract.

sentence: Sir, it has appeared to us that we are at liberty to pursue one or other of three courses with respect to the institution of Maynooth.
haiku_v2_rationale: Parliament is empowered to choose among three courses of action regarding Maynooth, a concrete option being granted/enabled to the legislative body itself.

sentence: Anyone who is interested in the liberty of the individual and the development of rights knows the posisition.
haiku_v2_rationale: The individual is being freed from interference (constraint on their agency), framed as a foundational principle of liberal governance—liberty as a value opposed to state control.

sentence: Her whole life seemed to be a series of acts of charity—here, interceding with the Protestant masters or mistresses of some of the Roman Catholic witnesses at her trial, that they might have liberty of fasting or attending mass;
haiku_v2_rationale: The liberty being discussed is freedom from interference with religious practice (fasting and attending mass); this is freedom of religion in the negative sense—non-interference with conscience and worship.

sentence: Here again, it may be asked, what degree of freedom will there be in these elections?
haiku_v2_rationale: What degree of freedom (absence of constraint or coercion) will exist in conducting and participating in elections—freedom from manipulation, fraud, or external interference in the electoral process.

sentence:
Industrial civil servants, numbering nearly 400,000, are already allowed a very considerable measure of freedom as regards political


and civic activities;
haiku_v2_rationale: Industrial civil servants are allowed freedom regarding political and civic activities—freedom from interference/restriction in how they engage politically and civically.

sentence: Now, you must excuse my taking the liberty of reminding you that there is not a leading man on either front bench that  The Times,  guided with such wonderful ability, and wielding such a terrific power, has not by turns lauded and calumniated, nattered and vilified.
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "taking the liberty of reminding" is parliamentary procedure language—the speaker is using "liberty" politely to mean "permission" or "the opportunity to do something courteous," not making a substantive claim about freedom as a value. This is an incidental formulaic use, not a claim about liberty-as-a-value.

sentence: Those schools were usually in private ownership, and not subject to any trust, and the private owner was at liberty to do what he liked with his school.
haiku_v2_rationale: The private owner is freed from external constraint/regulation—at liberty to manage his own property (school) without interference from public authority or trust.

sentence: Notwithstanding all taunts, Her Majesty's Government would continue to carry out the policy which was contained in the Anglo-Turkish Convention, which he believed in his heart would do much to increase the power of England, to promote liberty in the East, and to secure our position alike in Asia and in Europe.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker claims the Convention will "promote liberty in the East" as a geopolitical objective—increasing England's power and securing its position. "Liberty" here denotes freed peoples or regions from oppression/foreign domination (negative liberty), but the sentence treats it as an outcome to be secured through state policy rather than a specific named freedom from interference. The object of freedom is vague (liberty generically, in the East), making it difficult to specify what exactly is being freed from or to.

sentence:
In 1941, as it was about to enter a world war and face enormous world pressure, Franklin Roosevelt said:
 In the future days which we seek to make secure we look forward to a world founded on four essential human freedoms.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "four essential human freedoms" as foundational values for a secure world, without specifying the content of those freedoms or whether they concern freedom from interference or capacity to act.

sentence: There have been two great crimes perpetrated against liberty.
haiku_v2_rationale: A rhetorical invocation of liberty as a value that has been attacked or wronged; the sentence does not specify what particular freedoms are at stake or whether it concerns freedom from interference or capacity to act.

sentence: It is not freedom for the local education authorities;
haiku_v2_rationale: Local education authorities are being denied capacity/empowerment to govern themselves or make decisions independently.

sentence: To echo the sentiments he told this House, it is the contribution we make in the belief in the values that British people have fought and died for — values of democracy, equality and freedom, fairness and tolerance — that make us British.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes "freedom" as a core British value that citizens have fought and died for, as part of a set of abstract national values (democracy, equality, fairness, tolerance). The freedom being referenced is freedom from tyranny and oppression — a foundational negative liberty claim embedded in a rhetorical appeal to shared values.

sentence: Was it possible to restrain the language of men and the freedom of the people to this extent?
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence asks whether it is possible to restrain "the freedom of the people," which refers to freedom from restraint or interference with their speech and expression—a negative liberty.

sentence: The theological colleges, through the Bishop of Gloucester, who knows them, of course, extremely well, roundly say that they will not remain in the University if these regulations controlling their freedom are enforced.
haiku_v2_rationale: The theological colleges object to regulations that control their freedom—the object being freed is their autonomous governance and institutional independence, and the freedom is negative liberty from regulatory interference in their affairs.

sentence: Friends on this side have spoken most eloquently about this erosion of liberty and the need to move most carefully and with considerable forethought in a wider inquiry.
haiku_v2_rationale: This sentence refers to "erosion of liberty" as a general concern about loss of freedom from interference, discussed as something to be cautious about in policy deliberation.

sentence: Before, however, I come to that part of the subject to which the amendment specially refers, I will take the liberty of adverting to some observations that have fallen from the Mover and Seconder of the Address.
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "take the liberty of" is a parliamentary procedural convention meaning to permit oneself to do something; it does not make a substantive claim about freedom as a political value.

sentence: I think it is fair to say that there has been some hesitation in the past in our embracing the issue of persecution of Christians — whether from a misguided concern about our history and the role of missionaries, I do not know — but now is the time when we have to put all that behind us and say that freedom of religious belief is an essential and indivisible part of freedom, full stop.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker identifies "freedom of religious belief" as essential to freedom itself, opposing interference with or persecution of Christians' right to hold religious convictions.

sentence: We are seeking to create a unity of Government, managers and workers which will bring our nation once again, and soon, I hope, to economic freedom and prosperity.
haiku_v2_rationale: The nation is being freed to (or toward) economic self-sufficiency and prosperity through unified action—an enabling capacity rather than removal of constraint.

sentence: All these people have had four or five months of liberty and equality and fraternisation, which always follows any form of revolution.
haiku_v2_rationale: These people have experienced freedom from the constraints of the old social order, experiencing liberation from hierarchical control and oppression through revolutionary change.

sentence: However freedom of information requests are dealt with, it is still important that as well as having tough rules and robust audit, we have a scheme to put in the public domain, without its having been asked for, information about what all Members are spending every year, and on what.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of information (requests and public disclosure of spending data) is freedom FROM interference with access to government records and parliamentary spending information.

sentence: So far as the question of expenses is concerned, there is nothing in it whatever, nor is there anything in the suggestion which is uncharitably made, and those of us who believe in maintaining the liberty of the subject and object to these statutory tribunals, see no actual prospect of more litigation than exists under the ordinary law.
haiku_v2_rationale: The liberty of the subject is being defended against interference from statutory tribunals—the speaker invokes liberty as opposition to arbitrary procedural constraint on individual legal standing and remedy.

sentence: To what extent should the Government be the arbiters of fate for the individual, and to what extent should the Government interfere with the liberty of people in such matters as this?
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence contrasts government arbitration of fate versus non-interference with liberty, asking about the proper scope of government constraint on individual choice—a classic negative liberty concern about freedom from state interference.

sentence: She will also be well aware that there is a long-standing debate in Northern Ireland about the freedom to set corporation tax rates, which would involve a different arrangement from the one that we have now.
haiku_v2_rationale: Northern Ireland's empowerment to set its own corporation tax rates—a capacity to govern fiscal policy autonomously rather than be constrained by Westminster's current arrangement.

sentence:
The Bill is about the granting of freedom and independence to over 5 million people, the majority of whom have been deprived of freedom, rights and

opportunities for generations.
haiku_v2_rationale: Being freed is the capacity for over 5 million people to exercise independence and self-governance (what was previously denied them); this is positive liberty—an empowerment to act as a free people, not merely removal of a single specific constraint.

sentence: The simple fact was, that success no longer beamed so decisively on the cause of liberty in the Peninsula.
haiku_v2_rationale: Liberty is invoked as a political cause opposed to (presumably) tyranny or foreign domination in the Peninsula—freedom from external constraint or oppressive rule.

sentence: The Prime Minister also said that our democracy in our Westminster Parliament was the root of our freedom of choice — that was the essence of what he was saying.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of choice is a negative liberty: citizens are freed from interference in making their own choices, and Westminster Parliament's democratic structure protects that non-interference.

sentence: and he, as an Irishman, declared that it was in the interests of the freedom and peace of his country that this Act should remain in force, and that it should be in the power of any Government to put its provisions, when the necessity arose into operation.
haiku_v2_rationale: The freedom of the country (Ireland) to act through government power to implement necessary provisions—being freed/empowered to exercise governmental authority.

sentence: For instance, the liberty of the press in this country, the freedom of debate in this House, is probably very injurious to Spain, and to every other despotic government;
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence refers to "liberty of the press" and "freedom of debate"—both are instances of freedom from interference with specific communicative acts, in contrast to despotic government's censorship and suppression.

sentence: I should hate this Government to be blamed for destroying what should be an integral part of any civilised society and of the policies of any party that values freedom of speech, freedom of thought and freedom of the written word.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom from interference with speech, thought, and the written word—classic negative liberties protecting individuals and society from governmental censorship and control.

sentence: But we were asked last night whether the Government are not at liberty to give advice to Foreign Powers;
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "at liberty to" here is a parliamentary procedural idiom meaning "permitted" or "entitled," not a substantive claim about freedom as a value. It refers to what the Government is procedurally allowed to do in conduct of diplomacy, not freedom from interference or empowerment to achieve capacity.

sentence: I am glad to hear it is provided for, that some provision for religious liberty is made.
haiku_v2_rationale: Religious liberty is freedom from interference with one's religious beliefs and practices—a classic negative liberty protecting conscience and worship against state or social coercion.

sentence: Since the Union, Ireland has grievously declined, both in prosperity and political freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: Ireland's political freedom (capacity to self-govern) has declined since the Union—a loss of positive liberty, i.e., diminished empowerment to determine its own political institutions and affairs.

sentence: Gentleman is saying that allowing people to advertise weapons in a way that incites violence is protecting freedom of speech.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of speech is freedom from interference with expression; the sentence invokes "freedom of speech" as a negative liberty claim against restrictions on advertising weapons.

sentence: asked the Minister of Food whether, in view of the fact that herring fishermen have sustained serious losses since the season opened in April and that the Herring Industry Board have been compelled to abandon their controlled schemes, he will restore complete freedom to the industry by withdrawing price controls.
haiku_v2_rationale: The herring industry is being freed from government price controls, which is freedom from state interference in market operations and trade decisions.

sentence: They were of opinion that it would be more to the advantage and promotion of civil and religious liberty that the Church should be content with its present position, because, although it appeared by the Bill that the new bishops were not to have seats in the other House, yet he saw nothing in the Bill to prevent their being called up to the House of Lords at a future time.
haiku_v2_rationale: Civil and religious liberty is being promoted by allowing the Church to remain in its present position and not gain additional powers through new bishops having seats or future appointments to the House of Lords—this is negative liberty, freedom from the Church's institutional expansion and interference with secular authority.

sentence: I read an article last week in a Sunday newspaper in which it was said that there are no D Notices or Official Secrets Act in that heavenly country, in the land of freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom from state censorship and official secrecy laws (D Notices, Official Secrets Act)—a rhetorical invocation of liberty as absence of government interference with information and expression.

sentence: no change except in the convictions of the Government, who are now willing to swallow all their old convictions, and to endeavour to impose still more binding fetters on the freedom of discussion in Committee of Supply.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence opposes "fetters on the freedom of discussion" — invoking freedom from governmental interference with parliamentary debate and discussion.

sentence: Schools have found that, having acquired that freedom, they want to keep it.
haiku_v2_rationale: Schools have acquired the freedom (capacity/empowerment) to govern themselves and manage their own operations, and they want to retain that enabling power.

sentence: they have acknowledged from the first that we have entire fiscal liberty.
haiku_v2_rationale: Nations or territories are empowered to conduct their own fiscal/taxation policy without external interference, an enabling capacity to self-govern in financial matters.

sentence:
When he announced his proposals, he used these words:
 But I should warn industry generally that if there is any further breaking away, I shall consider myself at liberty to introduce legislation to restrict dividends in the next Finance Bill.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker is claiming authority to act (introduce legislation) if a condition arises. "At liberty to" is a parliamentary/formal phrase meaning "empowered to" or "authorized to" in this context—the freedom being discussed is the speaker's own capacity to legislate, not freedom from interference.

sentence: Trial by jury was the best security for the freedom of that house;
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of Parliament (the House) from undue interference in its proceedings; trial by jury is a mechanism protecting this institutional freedom from arbitrary action.

sentence: One can imagine the freedom of discussion in the Cabinet.
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "freedom of discussion" names freedom from interference with debate or deliberation within the Cabinet.

sentence: in reply, said, the noble Lord was at perfect liberty to apply to the Sheriff of Wigtonshire;
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "at liberty to apply" is parliamentary procedural language referring to permission or eligibility to take an action, not a substantive claim about freedom as a political value.

sentence: " and in other places he says it means "a certain freedom and independence conveyed by Royal Charter.
haiku_v2_rationale: A Royal Charter conveys freedom and independence — a positive capacity or right of self-governance/autonomy granted to a body or jurisdiction.

sentence: That house was the guardian of the liberty and property of the people;
haiku_v2_rationale: The House of Parliament is characterised as guardian protecting the people's liberty and property from interference or violation—a classic formulation of negative liberty.

sentence: The Bill violated the most fundamental principles of religious liberty.
haiku_v2_rationale: Religious liberty is freedom from state interference with conscience and worship; the sentence criticises violation of freedom from such interference.

sentence: In the main, I have high regard for Liberty briefings, even though I sometimes I disagree with them, but this one is littered with unfounded assertions and important inaccuracies.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence refers to "Liberty briefings" (briefings from Liberty, the civil rights organization) and discusses their content quality, not a substantive claim about liberty-as-a-value or freedom from/to anything.

sentence: He referred to freedom of contract.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of contract names the freedom from interference with parties' ability to make binding agreements.

sentence: We see its generous influence pervading the earlier labours of the Long Parliament, till that unfortunate conflict arose which, though it humbled the pride of kings, disgraced the cause of freedom, and closed the eventful scene in tyranny, in outrage, and in blood.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom is being opposed to tyranny, outrage, and blood as a rhetorical invocation of liberty as a cause against despotism and violence.

sentence: I say that this is as gross an interference with the freedom of the press as over occurred in Ireland, and that is saying a great deal.
haiku_v2_rationale: The press is being freed from interference—specifically from censorship or coercive restrictions on publication. This invokes freedom from governmental or institutional obstruction of the press's ability to publish.

sentence: " But if the fire goes on it it not a question of thinking who is to blame, it is a question of all hands to assist, and because we love our country and believe in its future, because we believe in democracy and liberty, not only here but throughout the world, we say to the Government that if you are really going forward to put out the fire now we are bound to give you our help.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes "democracy and liberty" as a cause that motivates national unity against threats ("the fire"), using liberty rhetorically as a value opposed to its destruction—a classic negative liberty invocation (freedom from tyranny/oppression).

sentence: I appreciate the recent announcements, but can the Minister reassure us that all universities will be able to access those loans, with freedom to invest in line with local priorities?
haiku_v2_rationale: Universities freed (empowered) to invest in line with local priorities—capacity to decide their own investment strategy, not freedom from interference with existing investments.

sentence: When they are sold as prostitutes they are made to work in appalling conditions, with no freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: Victims of trafficking are freed from forced labor and exploitation—a negative liberty from interference with bodily autonomy and choice.

sentence: The letter written to the prisoner was as follows:—"Dear Sir, excuse the liberty I take in writing to you: I hope this letter may find yourself and Mrs.
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "take the liberty of" is a courteous formulaic expression asking permission to do something (here, to write). It is parliamentary/epistolary procedure, not a substantive claim about freedom as a value.

sentence:
If the noble marquis meant, that no measure having for its object to abridge the liberty of the subject ought ever to be adopted without their lordships
 138 
being put in possession of sufficient grounds to justify such a proceeding, he perfectly agreed with him in that proposition.
haiku_v2_rationale: What is being protected: the liberty/freedom of the subject from abridgement (interference with their rights). The normative claim is against measures that would restrict this freedom without proper justification.

sentence: The Economist believes that there should be bounds to the freedom of directors to do what they like.
haiku_v2_rationale: Directors' capacity to act without constraint (to do what they like) is being bounded or restricted, involving freedom from interference with their discretionary actions.

sentence: It needs to be clearly understood in Departments, public bodies and elsewhere that if that freedom is found to be threatened, it will be protected.
haiku_v2_rationale: An unspecified freedom (not named) is being protected from threat or interference—the assertion is that non-interference with this freedom will be secured and defended.

sentence: If it is contrary to the policy of his Department to limit their freedom to engage labour in any way, why does the Department use the Fair Wages Clause, and why do they lay down conditions for local authorities who engage unemployed workers?
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom to engage labour (hire workers) — what is being constrained is the capacity or right to hire; the speaker is questioning why the Department limits this freedom through Fair Wages Clauses and conditions, framing the issue as constraint on employers' freedom to manage their labour practices.

sentence: No country on earth has had so many minds, for such a series of years, engaged in such contemplations, and in attempts by every species of arrangement, moral, legal, and political, to increase the liberty and happiness of the people.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker credits arrangements and attempts aimed to "increase the liberty and happiness" of the people through various institutional and legal means—this describes efforts to expand the capacities and well-being of the population, which is the empowering sense of positive liberty.

sentence: Many controls are not restrictive but, rather, are productive of even greater freedom for other people.
haiku_v2_rationale: Controls are framed as enabling productive capacity—"productive of even greater freedom for other people"—through constraint or regulation, representing freedom as an achieved state of empowerment rather than absence of interference.

sentence: but, taking it altogether, the country enjoys as much practical liberty, and parliament is really as independent, as in any former reign.
haiku_v2_rationale: The country and parliament are being freed from constraint (tyranny/interference in governance), measured by de facto "practical liberty" and independence—a negative liberty claim about freedom from external interference with sovereign independence.

sentence: They are always serious and earnest in coming forward to expose their lives and fortunes in the just and necessary contest in which their country may be engaged, and when the constitution and liberty of the country are endangered.
haiku_v2_rationale: The "liberty of the country" is threatened and endangered (negative liberty—freedom from foreign domination and despotism).

sentence: After all it should be remembered that liberty of discussion, even when it had amounted to license, bad resulted in making discussion profitable.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of discussion (the object) from interference or restriction, even when excessive; the speaker argues that this liberty—freedom from constraints on debate—has produced beneficial results.
